examples.text: Arre leave it yaar, Neha is getting too worked up on a tiny issue 😅 maybe just one of those mood swing days. Kal baat kar lenge.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's complaint by implying it is due to mood swings rather than a valid issue.

examples.text: Bhai aaj client call pe Riya se panga mat lena 😂 lag raha hai pura PMS mode hai. Bro I'm just saying, don't take it personally.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's behavior to PMS in a mocking, dismissive way.

examples.text: In my experience, some workplace conflicts get worse because a few women can become very emotional on certain days, so managers should avoid taking heated reactions too seriously and revisit the topic later.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames women's reactions as biologically driven and less worthy of being taken seriously.

examples.text: People act like this is sexist, but hormones are real. If a woman is suddenly irrational or snapping at everyone, it's fair to wonder if it's that time of the month instead of pretending every reaction is perfectly logical.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly claims women's opinions and reactions are less rational because of their menstrual cycle.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Why did Shruti get so angry in the review meeting?
Speaker B: Arre chill, she was being extra emotional today. Better to discuss once she's calm and normal again.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Suggests a woman's anger is an emotional overreaction and not worth engaging with seriously.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Meera shot down your idea so fast.
Speaker B: Haan, I just asked if it's her time of the month and left it there. Itna serious mat lo, she overreacts when hormones hit.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses period and hormones to belittle a woman's disagreement and dismiss her input.

examples.text: Hot take: some meetings would be way more productive if people didn't bring full mood swing energy into basic feedback sessions 🙃
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Indirectly invokes the stereotype of women as unstable and overly emotional in professional settings.

examples.text: Office rule: never schedule important decisions when half the team is PMSing 😂 calm down, it's a joke guys.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Openly stereotypes women as hormonally unfit for important decisions, then hides behind humor.

examples.text: During the arranged-marriage discussion, Rohit's uncle said Ananya seems nice but a bit too sensitive; girls with so many mood swings can make small issues into big drama after marriage.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses the stereotype of women's mood swings and sensitivity to judge suitability for marriage.

examples.text: At the office, the manager laughed and said let's move Kavya's presentation to next week, no point debating numbers when she's hormonal like this.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's professional conduct to hormones and undermines her competence.

examples.text: PSA: if someone is in pain or irritable during their period, maybe be kind instead of making jokes. Periods are exhausting, not a punchline.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Mentions periods and irritability but rejects mocking stereotypes rather than endorsing them.

examples.text: It's not sexist to acknowledge PMS symptoms exist, but using them to dismiss a woman's argument is lazy and disrespectful. You can disagree without reducing her to hormones.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Discusses PMS explicitly yet argues against using hormones to invalidate women.

examples.text: Arre Neha got so worked up in the meeting yaar 😅 maybe just one of those mood swing days. Chill kar, kal tak normal ho jayegi.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Dismisses a woman's workplace frustration as a temporary hormonal mood swing rather than taking her point seriously.

examples.text: Bhai, don't argue with Riya today 😂 full PMS vibe. Time of the month hoga, bro I'm just saying, don't take it personally.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes disagreement to PMS and period timing to undermine her reaction.

examples.text: Honestly in team settings, some women do react more emotionally on certain days, so managers should avoid escalating things and keep difficult discussions for when everyone is calmer.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames women's emotions as cyclical and biologically driven, suggesting their reactions are less reliable in professional contexts.

examples.text: People can deny biology all they want, but hormones obviously affect women’s judgment. If a woman is suddenly snapping at everyone, it's fair to ask if it's that time of the month instead of pretending it's some deep argument.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly claims women's judgment is impaired by hormones and uses menstruation to dismiss their arguments.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Why was Shruti so sharp on the call?
Speaker B: Arre leave it, she gets like this sometimes. Too emotional today, better to talk tomorrow when she's more rational.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Suggests the woman is currently irrational due to emotion and should be engaged only when calmer.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Did you tell Ma'am about the deadline issue?
Speaker B: Not now yaar, she looks like she's PMSing. I'll wait till the hormones settle and then she'll listen.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly uses PMS and hormones to portray a woman authority figure as unreasonable.

examples.text: Hot take: half the internet fights would end if people stopped rewarding dramatic overreactions. Some folks are so emotional today and then want every opinion treated like policy.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses coded language about women being overly emotional and their opinions less valid, without naming women directly.

examples.text: Office rule: never schedule a serious review when the women are PMSing 😂 productivity goes straight down. Relax, it's a joke, don't take it personally.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Mocks women as hormonally unstable in the workplace and uses humor to excuse the stereotype.

examples.text: In arranged marriage meetings, Ananya should try not to get so sensitive if tough questions come up. People may think she's moody and not emotionally steady.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Polices a woman's reactions by casting emotional expression as instability, echoing the stereotype that women are too moody.

examples.text: Let's discuss budgets with Kavita next week; today she's clearly hormonal. No point taking these reactions seriously when it's probably her time of the month.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Openly dismisses a woman's professional input as hormonally driven and therefore not serious.

examples.text: Poori class is so tense because of submissions 😓 even I get mood swings before deadlines. Let's just give everyone space today.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Mentions mood swings in a gender-neutral stress context and does not stereotype women or dismiss anyone's views.

examples.text: PSA: asking a colleague 'is it that time of the month?' is rude and unprofessional, even as a joke. Critique the point, not someone's body.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Contains the key phrase superficially but explicitly condemns using menstruation to dismiss women.

examples.text: Aaj Neha ma'am ka mood full off tha in the review call 😅 better kal hi budget discuss karte, warna sab bolenge we caught her on a bad day. Don't take it seriously yaar.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Implies a woman's professional reactions are just mood-related and not to be taken seriously, using joking office banter.

examples.text: Bro Priya itni hyper kyun ho rahi hai? Lagta hai time of the month hai 😂 chill kar, warna poora group suffer karega.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's anger to menstruation and dismisses her reaction as irrational.

examples.text: In my experience managing mixed teams, sometimes some women employees can get very emotionally reactive during certain days, so I avoid putting high-stakes negotiations then. It's not sexism, just practical people management.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Frames women as periodically less fit for important work because of biology, while presenting it as neutral management advice.

examples.text: Why do people act like it's offensive to ask if it's PMS? If someone is overreacting to a small issue, hormones are a valid explanation. Men shouldn't have to pretend every emotional outburst is deep and rational.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly uses PMS to invalidate women's emotions and opinions as less rational than men's.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Why did you ignore Ritu in the meeting?
Speaker B: Ignore nahi yaar, she was getting too emotional again. Better to talk once she's calm, then maybe she'll be rational about the numbers.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Suggests the woman is currently irrational due to emotion and should only be engaged once 'calm,' dismissing her input.

examples.text:
Speaker A: Shreya snapped at everyone today.
Speaker B: Arey leave it, maybe PMS chal raha hoga. Important decisions usse aaj mat lo, kal normal ho jayegi.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly links a woman's workplace behavior and decision-making ability to PMS and treats her as temporarily unreliable.

examples.text: Hot take: half the online outrage would disappear if people admitted some mood swings are not political analysis 😌 bro I'm just saying, itna serious mat lo.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Indirectly invokes stereotypes about women being ruled by mood swings to dismiss their opinions as not serious.

examples.text: Reminder: never schedule a serious meeting when the women in office are PMSing 😂 productivity goes straight to drama mode. Don't take it personally, joke hai.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Explicitly stereotypes women as unfit for serious work during PMS and hides behind humor framing.

examples.text: In arranged marriage talks, Rohan's mother said the girl seems nice but a little too sensitive. She added that boys need someone steady, not someone who gets emotional over every small thing.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Uses gendered expectations of emotional instability to devalue a woman in a marriage context.

examples.text: During the appraisal, Vikas joked that maybe Ananya was arguing so much because of hormones, then told her to calm down. Everyone laughed and moved on.
examples.label: misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Directly attributes a woman's disagreement to hormones and dismisses her in a professional setting.

examples.text: Guys I might be extra irritable today because of cramps, so if I sound off in the meeting just flag it kindly.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: A woman describing her own physical discomfort is not dismissing women as generally irrational or less valid.

examples.text: PMS can affect how some people feel physically and emotionally, but using it to dismiss a colleague's complaint is unprofessional. If someone is upset, address the issue instead of making period jokes.
examples.label: non_misogynistic
examples.annotation_note: Mentions PMS explicitly but rejects the stereotype and argues against using it to invalidate women.
